title: Lambdas and captures

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C++: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Lambdas and captures; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A count_if lambda captures threshold by value so later changes to the outer variable do not alter the predicate. Your goal is to implement and trace value and reference captures in an algorithm call. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Iterators and invalidation; Overloading and default arguments; Standard algorithms; Value, reference and const-reference parameters. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: A lambda creates a closure object whose capture list determines copied or referenced state, parameter list defines calls, and mutable affects copied capture modification. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Reference captures can dangle, [=] can copy more context than intended, this capture rules evolve, and closure types are unique unnamed types. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: A lambda using [&] safely keeps every referenced local alive for as long as the lambda object exists.

includes: Define local callables and capture only intended state with explicit lifetime reasoning.
Implement and trace value and reference captures in an algorithm call.
excludes: Reference captures can dangle, [=] can copy more context than intended, this capture rules evolve, and closure types are unique unnamed types.

outcomes: Implement and trace value and reference captures in an algorithm call.

examples.title: Lambdas and captures: defined case
examples.situation: A count_if lambda captures threshold by value so later changes to the outer variable do not alter the predicate.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A count_if lambda captures threshold by value so later changes to the outer variable do not alter the predicate.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Reference capture does not extend local lifetime; ensure every referenced object outlives all closure calls or capture an owned value.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Reference capture does not extend local lifetime; ensure every referenced object outlives all closure calls or capture an owned value.

examples.title: Lambdas and captures: boundary case
examples.situation: A callback that outlives its creating scope avoids reference capture of a local variable that would dangle.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A callback that outlives its creating scope avoids reference capture of a local variable that would dangle.
Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Reference captures can dangle, [=] can copy more context than intended, this capture rules evolve, and closure types are unique unnamed types.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: A lambda using [&] safely keeps every referenced local alive for as long as the lambda object exists.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement and trace value and reference captures in an algorithm call.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: A lambda using [&] safely keeps every referenced local alive for as long as the lambda object exists.
misconceptions.correction: Reference capture does not extend local lifetime; ensure every referenced object outlives all closure calls or capture an owned value.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: A lambda using [&] safely keeps every referenced local alive for as long as the lambda object exists.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model Lambdas and captures: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
